title: Stackable Fruit & Vegetable Storage Crates
summary: A slatted 1x2 crate with a loose, unfastened bottom you can pull out to dump and rinse — built from a single board and a bundle of wood lath.
description:
This is the crate you build five of, not one — the whole point is stacking a produce shelf's worth of identical bins from the cheapest materials in the lumber aisle: one 1x2 and a bundle of wood lath (the same thin strips used for plaster backing, sold in bulk and nearly free per piece).

The legs and bottom-support frame get pocket holes and glue, but everything above that — the end slats, side slats, and bottom slats — is nailed with 3/4" brads, because a produce crate takes daily handling and brad-and-glue joints are fast to build in volume and easy to replace a board on if one splits.

The bottom slats are the one deliberate exception to "glue everything": they sit loose inside the frame, unfastened. Pull them out to dump wilted lettuce or rinse dirt off potatoes, then drop them back in. A glued-in bottom would turn every cleaning into a scraping job.

Build the end slats first, then cut the side slats to fit the actual box you built rather than to a fixed number — lath thickness varies enough between pieces that "cut to fit" beats a spec measurement here. Stack a few crates and the 2-1/2" starting gap above the legs (called out in the cut sequence) is what lets one crate nest cleanly on the one below it.

tools.note: Every slat — end, side, and bottom.

materials.name: 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs and bottom supports, per crate.

materials.name: Wood lath, 4 ft pieces
materials.note: Sold in bulk bundles of ~50 — this is the per-crate usage. 1x2 scrap works as a substitute.

cutList.note: Joins the legs at the bottom, pocket-holed.
Cut to fit the assembled box rather than to this exact length — lath dimensions vary.
Loose, unfastened — lifts out for cleaning.

steps.title: Build the leg frame
steps.body: Drill pocket holes in the bottom of the two longer 1x2 support pieces and attach them to the four legs with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue, forming a low rectangular frame that stands on four short feet.

steps.title: Attach the end slats
steps.body: Nail the six end slats to the frame with 3/4" brads and glue, starting 2-1/2" up from the bottom of the legs — that gap is what lets one crate nest into the top of another when you stack them.

steps.title: Cut and attach the side slats
steps.body: With the end slats holding the legs together, measure your actual box and cut the side slats to fit rather than to a fixed length. Nail on the same way, brads and glue.

steps.title: Drop in the bottom slats
steps.body: Set the eight bottom slats inside the crate, resting on the bottom supports. Leave them unfastened — the whole point is being able to lift them out to dump and rinse the crate without disassembling anything.

images.alt: Small slatted wood crate for stacking fruit and vegetable storage
